_meta.authoringNotes: Cross-checked against the AAO Clinical Statement on orbital hemorrhage, BMJ 2014 review on lateral canthotomy and cantholysis, Ballard 2009 retrobulbar review, and AAOMS 2011 orbital compartment syndrome consensus. Time-to-treatment numbers are from Hayreh, Liu, Lima, and Sun cohort series.

h1Title: Orbital Hemorrhage: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Orbital hemorrhage is bleeding inside the bony eye socket that, when it accumulates behind the globe, raises intraorbital pressure within minutes and can blind a patient through ischemic optic neuropathy. Most cases follow blunt facial trauma, midface fractures, or orbital surgery; iatrogenic bleeding occurs in 0.04-3% of retrobulbar anesthesia injections and about 1 in 25,000 cosmetic blepharoplasties. The hallmark triad is sudden severe pain, tense proptosis, and vision loss with an afferent pupillary defect. Expanding retrobulbar hemorrhage is a true ophthalmic emergency: irreversible retinal and optic nerve ischemia begins within 60-90 minutes once orbital pressure crosses retinal perfusion pressure. Immediate bedside lateral canthotomy and inferior cantholysis decompresses the orbit and preserves sight when performed in time.

keyStats.prevalence: Retrobulbar hemorrhage complicates 0.04-3% of retrobulbar/peribulbar anesthesia and approximately 1 in 25,000 blepharoplasties (AAO Clinical Statement; Hass 2004)
keyStats.demographics: Trauma-related cases skew young male (15-45 years); spontaneous cases skew older with anticoagulation, hypertension, or vascular malformations
keyStats.avgAge: Bimodal: trauma cluster age 20-40, iatrogenic and spontaneous cluster age 55+
keyStats.globalCases: True incidence undocumented; estimated tens of thousands annually worldwide given trauma volume and oculoplastic surgical case load

definition: Orbital hemorrhage (ICD-10: H05.23) is bleeding within the bony orbital compartment that may involve any of four anatomic spaces: subperiosteal, intraconal (inside the muscle cone, the site of sight-threatening retrobulbar hematoma), extraconal, and subconjunctival/preseptal. The orbit is a closed pyramidal cavity bounded by the orbital walls posteriorly and the orbital septum and globe anteriorly, so any expanding collection rapidly elevates intraorbital pressure. When pressure exceeds central retinal artery perfusion pressure (approximately 80 mmHg in a normotensive adult), retinal and optic nerve perfusion collapses and ischemic optic neuropathy follows within 60-90 minutes. This pathophysiology, called orbital compartment syndrome (OCS), is the reason orbital hemorrhage is treated as a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate intervention rather than imaging. Causes span trauma (blunt, penetrating, midface fracture), iatrogenic injury during retrobulbar block or oculoplastic surgery, spontaneous bleeding from anticoagulation, hypertension, valsalva, or orbital vascular malformations, and rare neoplastic vascular tumors. The condition is co-managed by ophthalmology, oculoplastics, emergency medicine, oral and maxillofacial surgery, and otolaryngology depending on mechanism and setting.

typesClassification.description: Bleeding inside the muscle cone behind the globe. The most dangerous variant because pressure transmits directly to the optic nerve and retinal vessels. Source is typically a deep orbital branch of the ophthalmic artery or an inferior ophthalmic vein.

Blood accumulates between the orbital bone and the periorbita, most often along the roof in pediatric trauma or after sinus surgery. Can displace the globe but usually does not raise intraorbital pressure as rapidly as retrobulbar bleeding.
Bleeding outside the rectus muscle cone within the orbital fat. May follow trauma or surgery; less likely to compress the optic nerve directly but can still cause significant proptosis.

Anterior to the orbital septum or under the bulbar conjunctiva. Often dramatic in appearance but usually self-limited and does not threaten vision. Important to distinguish from deeper, sight-threatening bleeding.

primarySymptoms: Sudden, severe, deep aching pain behind the affected eye, often described as a pressure or bursting sensation that worsens within minutes of onset.
Visible proptosis, with the affected eye protruding several millimeters compared with the fellow eye and a tense, woody resistance on attempted retropulsion.
Periorbital ecchymosis and swelling, frequently with bilateral 'raccoon eyes' when associated with naso-orbito-ethmoid or basilar skull fracture.
Rapidly progressive decreased visual acuity, ranging from blurred vision to no light perception within hours if the optic nerve is compromised.
Loss of color saturation, especially red desaturation, which is one of the earliest signs of optic nerve ischemia and often precedes acuity drop.
Restricted extraocular movements in all directions of gaze with diplopia, caused by mechanical tethering and orbital pressure on the rectus muscles.
Elevated intraocular pressure, typically above 40 mmHg by tonometry and sometimes exceeding 60 mmHg, reflecting the transmitted orbital compartment pressure.
A relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) on the affected side, indicating asymmetric optic nerve dysfunction that mandates urgent decompression.
Subconjunctival hemorrhage and chemosis (edematous conjunctiva) overlying the globe, sometimes severe enough that the conjunctiva balloons outside the lid margin.
Nausea, vomiting, and bradycardia in severe cases, driven by the oculocardiac reflex and rising orbital pressure.
earlyWarningSigns: Increasing periorbital pain after eyelid surgery, retrobulbar block, or facial trauma that exceeds expected post-procedure discomfort
Progressive eyelid swelling and tightness during the first 6-24 hours after orbital surgery or significant facial injury
New onset of color desaturation, especially red, in the affected eye even before measurable visual acuity loss
Tense palpation of the globe and resistance to gentle retropulsion compared with the unaffected side
A patient on anticoagulation who develops sudden unilateral eye pain with proptosis, even in the absence of trauma
emergencySigns: Vision loss or color desaturation with proptosis and a tense globe — proceed directly to lateral canthotomy and inferior cantholysis without waiting for imaging
Intraocular pressure above 40 mmHg with optic nerve symptoms — an immediate sign of orbital compartment syndrome
A new relative afferent pupillary defect on the affected side after trauma, surgery, or retrobulbar injection
Sudden no-light-perception vision in an eye that previously had useful vision, with the eye visibly proptotic and tense
Bradycardia, nausea, or vomiting accompanying severe orbital pain — reflects elevated orbital pressure and triggers the oculocardiac reflex

causes.description: Direct blows from assault, motor vehicle collision, sports impact, or falls can shear deep orbital vessels and produce intraconal bleeding. Often coexists with orbital wall fractures, including blowout fractures of the floor or medial wall.
Naso-orbito-ethmoid, Le Fort II and III, and zygomaticomaxillary complex fractures disrupt small orbital vessels. Subperiosteal hematomas are particularly common after roof or medial wall fractures.

Needle injury to deep orbital vessels during local anesthesia for cataract or vitreoretinal surgery causes hemorrhage in 0.04-3% of injections. Higher with retrobulbar than peribulbar technique and with longer or sharper needles.

Blepharoplasty, dacryocystorhinostomy, orbital decompression, lacrimal surgery, and orbital tumor resection all carry hemorrhage risk. Blepharoplasty hemorrhage is rare (about 1 in 25,000 cases) but is the most commonly cited cause of post-cosmetic blindness.

Warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants, dual antiplatelet therapy, and therapeutic heparin increase spontaneous and trauma-related orbital bleeding. Reversal of anticoagulation is part of the management once decompression is initiated.

Sudden venous pressure spikes can rupture fragile orbital varices or venous malformations and produce spontaneous hemorrhage. Cases have been reported after coughing fits, childbirth, weightlifting, and severe sneezing.

Lymphangioma, orbital varix, and arteriovenous malformation can rupture spontaneously or in response to minor trauma. Lymphangiomas in particular present with sudden 'chocolate cyst' hemorrhage in children and young adults.

Hemophilia, von Willebrand disease, leukemia, thrombocytopenia, and disseminated intravascular coagulation predispose to spontaneous orbital bleeding. Acute leukemia presenting with proptosis from orbital hemorrhage has been documented in children.

riskFactors.factor: Recent orbital, oculoplastic, or sinus surgery
riskFactors.description: Blepharoplasty, orbital decompression, DCR, and endoscopic sinus surgery all carry post-operative hemorrhage risk in the first 24-72 hours; risk persists longer with anticoagulation.
Needle-based orbital anesthesia for cataract or vitreoretinal surgery causes hemorrhage in 0.04-3% of injections. Sub-Tenon and topical techniques markedly reduce this risk.
Warfarin, DOACs, dual antiplatelet therapy, and unfractionated heparin increase both spontaneous and procedural bleeding. Risk is highest with supratherapeutic INR or recent loading doses.
Severe systolic hypertension can rupture orbital vessels spontaneously, particularly in older patients on antithrombotic therapy. Blood pressure control is part of post-event management.
Hemophilia, von Willebrand disease, thrombocytopenia, and acute leukemia raise spontaneous orbital bleeding risk; orbital hemorrhage can be the presenting feature of new-onset leukemia in children.
Orbital varix, lymphangioma, and arteriovenous malformation are anatomically predisposed to rupture with valsalva, minor trauma, or hormonal shifts.
Motor vehicle occupants without seat belts, contact-sport athletes without protective eyewear, assault victims, and combat personnel face the highest trauma-related risk.
Trauma-related orbital hemorrhage cohorts skew strongly male and young adult due to disproportionate exposure to interpersonal violence, motor vehicle collisions, and contact sports.

affectedDemographics: Trauma series report 70-85% male predominance among assault and motor vehicle collision cases
Pediatric orbital hemorrhage is most often subperiosteal and follows roof fractures, sinusitis-related vascular injury, or underlying lymphangioma
Post-blepharoplasty hemorrhage occurs at approximately 1 in 25,000 procedures, with about half causing permanent visual deficit if untreated (Hass 2004)
Among retrobulbar block recipients, reported hemorrhage rates range 0.04-3% depending on needle length, technique, and operator experience
Spontaneous orbital hemorrhage in older adults is over-represented among anticoagulated patients and those with uncontrolled hypertension
diagnosisOverview: Orbital hemorrhage is a clinical diagnosis. When a patient presents with the triad of proptosis, a tense globe, and decreased vision after trauma, surgery, retrobulbar block, or spontaneously, orbital compartment syndrome is assumed and treated before imaging. The AAO Clinical Statement and multiple trauma guidelines are explicit: in suspected OCS, decompression by lateral canthotomy and inferior cantholysis precedes the CT scanner. Initial assessment focuses on visual acuity (count fingers, hand motion, light perception), color vision, the swinging-flashlight test for a relative afferent pupillary defect, palpation for globe tension, measurement of intraocular pressure by tonometry, and a brief fundus check for central retinal artery occlusion, retinal hemorrhage, or disc edema. An IOP above 40 mmHg with vision loss after trauma or surgery is sufficient indication to intervene at the bedside. Once the orbit has been decompressed or if there is no immediate sight threat, non-contrast CT of the orbits is the imaging study of choice: it shows hyperdense intraconal or subperiosteal collections, posterior tenting of the globe, stretched optic nerve, and any associated fracture. MRI has a role in subacute hemorrhage and suspected vascular malformation. Laboratory work-up includes coagulation studies, INR, platelet count, and a complete blood count, especially in spontaneous cases. Septic causes (orbital cellulitis) must be excluded by temperature, infection markers, and imaging features; vascular masquerades such as cavernous sinus thrombosis and carotid-cavernous fistula present with proptosis but distinct pulsation, bruit, or systemic signs.

diagnosticTests.test: Visual acuity, color vision, and pupillary examination
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects optic nerve compromise. A relative afferent pupillary defect, dropped acuity, or red desaturation in a proptotic eye is sufficient to call orbital compartment syndrome and intervene immediately.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Reading an eye chart with each eye separately, viewing red and green color targets, and a swinging-flashlight test in a dim room. Takes 2-3 minutes at the bedside.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the transmitted orbital pressure. IOP above 40 mmHg in the setting of trauma or post-orbital surgery is a key threshold for proceeding with canthotomy and cantholysis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A Tono-Pen or iCare device applied briefly to the anesthetized cornea, or Goldmann applanation at the slit lamp. Takes under a minute and is painless after anesthetic drops.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the tense, woody resistance characteristic of expanding orbital hematoma and quantifies proptosis. Asymmetry of 2 mm or more between sides is significant.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner gently presses on the closed eyelid and measures globe protrusion with a Hertel exophthalmometer or millimeter ruler. Performed in seconds.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms intraconal, subperiosteal, or extraconal hemorrhage, characterizes any associated fracture, and identifies stretched optic nerve or posterior globe tenting that signals severe compartment pressure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Brief CT scan lasting 1-2 minutes, performed only after the eye has been decompressed or when vision is not immediately threatened. No contrast required for the initial study.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of the orbits with contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for subacute or recurrent cases, suspected lymphangioma or orbital varix, and when distinguishing hemorrhage age and source matters for surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20-30 minute scan with gadolinium contrast. Used after the acute emergency is controlled, typically as an outpatient or inpatient work-up.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects central retinal artery occlusion, central retinal vein occlusion, optic disc edema, and choroidal folds — downstream signs of orbital compartment pressure that change urgency and prognosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Examiner uses a direct or indirect ophthalmoscope after pupil dilation drops; takes 2-5 minutes per eye and may be deferred until after decompression in severe cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Coagulation panel, complete blood count, blood pressure
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies anticoagulation status, thrombocytopenia, or hematologic disease that may have provoked the bleed and that must be corrected as part of treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw with INR, aPTT, platelet count, hemoglobin, and white cell count; results in 30-60 minutes in most emergency departments.

treatmentOverview: Treatment depends entirely on whether the orbit is under compartment pressure threatening vision. When the triad of vision loss, proptosis, and a tense globe is present, or when IOP exceeds 40 mmHg with optic nerve symptoms, immediate lateral canthotomy and inferior cantholysis (LCIC) is performed at the bedside without waiting for imaging or specialist arrival. The procedure releases the lateral canthal tendon, which is the strongest tether of the orbital septum, and allows the globe and orbital contents to expand anteriorly, dropping orbital pressure within seconds. Visual recovery rates approach 80-90% when LCIC is performed within 60-90 minutes of vision loss and fall to near zero beyond 120 minutes (Liu 1993, Lima 2009). Adjunctive medical therapy supports decompression but does not replace it. Head elevation to 45 degrees, intravenous mannitol 20% at 1-2 g/kg, intravenous acetazolamide 500 mg, and topical IOP-lowering drops (timolol 0.5%, dorzolamide, brimonidine) lower intraocular pressure within minutes. Intravenous methylprednisolone 250-1000 mg has been used for optic nerve protection in some protocols, though high-level evidence is limited. Once orbital pressure is controlled, source control becomes the priority: anticoagulation is reversed with vitamin K, prothrombin complex concentrate, or specific reversal agents (idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitors); platelets are replaced if low; blood pressure is brought to normotensive range. Surgical drainage of organized or expanding hematoma via a transcutaneous or transconjunctival approach may follow if compartment pressure recurs or if a subperiosteal collection persists. Definitive management of the underlying cause — fracture repair, treatment of lymphangioma or varix, hematologic work-up — proceeds once the eye is safe. Ophthalmology and oculoplastic follow-up is essential to monitor optic nerve function, intraocular pressure, ocular motility, and the cosmetic outcome of canthotomy.

medicalTreatments.name: Lateral canthotomy and inferior cantholysis (bedside surgical decompression)
medicalTreatments.description: The definitive treatment for orbital compartment syndrome. Under local anesthetic, the lateral canthus is crushed with a hemostat and incised, then the inferior crus of the lateral canthal tendon is cut to release the orbital contents. Can be performed by trained emergency, trauma, or ophthalmology clinicians at the bedside in 2-5 minutes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 80-90% useful visual recovery when performed within 90 minutes of vision loss; near-zero recovery if delayed beyond 120 minutes with severe vision loss (Liu 1993, Lima 2009).

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous mannitol 20% (1-2 g/kg over 30 minutes)
medicalTreatments.description: Osmotic diuretic that lowers intraocular and orbital pressure rapidly. Used as adjunct to surgical decompression or while preparing for canthotomy. Avoid in severe heart failure, anuria, or significant electrolyte derangement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces IOP by 20-30 mmHg within 30-60 minutes in most patients; effect lasts 4-6 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous acetazolamide 500 mg
medicalTreatments.description: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that decreases aqueous humor production and lowers intraocular pressure. Used in combination with mannitol and topical drops as medical support around the time of surgical decompression. Dose-adjust in renal impairment and sulfa allergy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Drops IOP by 25-30% within 30-60 minutes; rarely sufficient as sole therapy in orbital compartment syndrome.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical IOP-lowering drops (timolol 0.5%, dorzolamide 2%, brimonidine 0.2%)
medicalTreatments.description: Combined topical therapy administered while preparing for canthotomy. Beta-blocker, carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, and alpha-2 agonist work by complementary mechanisms to reduce aqueous production and increase outflow.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Each agent reduces IOP by 15-25%; combination effect is additive within the first hour.

medicalTreatments.name: Anticoagulation reversal (vitamin K, PCC, idarucizumab, andexanet alfa, platelets, FFP)
medicalTreatments.description: Tailored to the offending agent: vitamin K and four-factor prothrombin complex concentrate for warfarin; idarucizumab for dabigatran; andexanet alfa for apixaban/rivaroxaban; platelet transfusion for antiplatelet therapy or thrombocytopenia; fresh frozen plasma when targeted reversal is unavailable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: INR normalizes within 30 minutes with four-factor PCC; dabigatran activity neutralized within 15 minutes by idarucizumab. Hemorrhage progression typically arrests once coagulation is corrected.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous methylprednisolone (250-1000 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in some protocols for adjunctive optic nerve protection after decompression, particularly when ischemic optic neuropathy is suspected. Evidence is limited to case series; routine use is not universally endorsed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Possible benefit on optic nerve recovery reported in observational series; no randomized trial demonstrates clear visual outcome improvement.

medicalTreatments.name: Surgical hematoma drainage (transcutaneous, transconjunctival, or transcaruncular)
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated when canthotomy and cantholysis do not adequately decompress the orbit, when a discrete subperiosteal collection is identified on CT, or when hemorrhage recurs. Performed in the operating room by ophthalmology or oculoplastics.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores normal orbital anatomy and prevents secondary compartment pressure in 80-90% of selected cases when combined with bony decompression as needed.

medicalTreatments.name: Blood pressure control (IV labetalol, nicardipine)
medicalTreatments.description: Aggressive but controlled lowering of systolic blood pressure into the normotensive range reduces the risk of ongoing or recurrent orbital bleeding. Target is generally systolic 120-140 mmHg in the acute window.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves target blood pressure within 15-30 minutes; reduces hematoma expansion risk in observational data.

surgicalOptions.name: Lateral canthotomy with inferior cantholysis
surgicalOptions.description: The emergency sight-saving procedure described above. Performed at the bedside under local anesthetic in 2-5 minutes; the lateral canthal tendon's inferior crus is divided to release orbital contents anteriorly. Repair of the canthus is delayed until the orbit is stable.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 80-90% useful visual recovery within 90 minutes of onset

surgicalOptions.name: Bony orbital decompression
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of medial or inferior orbital wall bone, performed when canthotomy and cantholysis do not control pressure or when extensive orbital hematoma persists. Approached transcaruncular, transconjunctival, or endoscopically through the ethmoid sinus by oculoplastics or otolaryngology.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective additional decompression in 90% of refractory cases; vision outcome depends on duration of pre-decompression ischemia.

surgicalOptions.name: Orbital exploration and hematoma evacuation
surgicalOptions.description: Direct evacuation of organized or expanding hematoma through transcutaneous, transconjunctival, or transcaruncular approach in the operating room. Used when imaging shows a discrete drainable collection or when bleeding recurs after initial decompression.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 80-90% relief of mass effect; combined with source vessel cautery when feasible.

surgicalOptions.name: Treatment of underlying vascular lesion
surgicalOptions.description: Embolization, sclerotherapy, or surgical excision of orbital varix, lymphangioma, or arteriovenous malformation that caused the bleed. Planned electively once the acute episode is stabilized.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Variable by lesion type; sclerotherapy of orbital lymphangioma reduces recurrent bleeding episodes in 70-80% of treated cases.

whySeeSpecialist: Any patient with suspected orbital hemorrhage and vision change, proptosis, or markedly elevated intraocular pressure should be evaluated immediately by ophthalmology or emergency medicine and, where time permits, by an oculoplastic surgeon. Specialist input is essential for canthotomy technique, bony decompression, evaluation of underlying vascular lesions, and post-event optic nerve surveillance. Even after successful decompression, ophthalmology follow-up over weeks to months monitors for delayed optic neuropathy, secondary glaucoma, motility deficits, and the cosmetic outcome of canthal reconstruction.
doctorSelectionGuide: In the acute setting, the closest emergency department with an on-call ophthalmologist or oculoplastic surgeon is the right destination — minutes matter. For longitudinal care, look for an oculoplastic surgeon (ASOPRS-trained in the US, equivalent fellowship credentials elsewhere) with experience in orbital trauma and decompression. Confirm access to high-resolution orbital imaging, ophthalmic anesthesia teams familiar with sub-Tenon and topical techniques, and a working relationship with hematology when anticoagulation or bleeding disorders are in play. Continuity matters for monitoring optic nerve recovery and managing any secondary glaucoma.
preventionStrategies: Prefer sub-Tenon or topical anesthesia over retrobulbar blocks for cataract and routine vitreoretinal surgery when clinically appropriate, eliminating the dominant iatrogenic cause
Stop antiplatelet and anticoagulant agents per surgical guidelines before elective oculoplastic and orbital surgery, with bridging individualized by thrombotic risk
Control systolic blood pressure below 140 mmHg in the perioperative period for any orbital, sinus, or facial surgery to reduce post-operative hemorrhage
Use protective eyewear in contact sports, industrial settings, and combat training to reduce orbital trauma incidence
Buckle up — properly worn three-point restraints reduce orbital and midface trauma in motor vehicle collisions by approximately 50%
Screen patients with known orbital varix, lymphangioma, or AVM for valsalva-triggering activities and counsel on early presentation if pain or proptosis recurs
lifestyleModifications: Avoid heavy lifting, straining, and valsalva for 1-2 weeks after orbital surgery or known orbital vascular malformation diagnosis
Adhere strictly to anticoagulation monitoring schedules and target INR ranges in patients on warfarin
Maintain blood pressure under 140/90 mmHg with daily home monitoring in patients with prior spontaneous orbital bleeding
Avoid contact sports for at least 4-6 weeks after orbital surgery or significant orbital trauma, then resume only with protective eyewear
Treat chronic sinusitis and allergic rhinitis actively to reduce sneezing and nose-blowing valsalva events in at-risk patients
Quit smoking — vascular health is implicated in spontaneous and post-traumatic orbital bleeding outcomes

recommended: DASH-style diet to support blood pressure control
Adequate hydration (1.5-2 L water daily) to support overall vascular health
Vitamin K-stable diet in patients on warfarin to maintain therapeutic INR
Iron-rich foods (lean red meat, leafy greens, lentils) during recovery from significant hemorrhage to support red blood cell recovery
Foods rich in vitamin C and bioflavonoids (citrus, berries) to support capillary integrity
avoid: Excess alcohol, which raises blood pressure and impairs platelet function
Very high sodium intake (>2.3 g/day) that worsens hypertension control
Sudden swings in leafy green intake on warfarin therapy, which destabilize INR
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice on calcium channel blockers and many anticoagulants due to CYP3A4 interactions
Unregulated herbal supplements with antiplatelet activity (ginkgo, garlic in high doses, ginseng, vitamin E megadoses)

exerciseGuidelines: Avoid all heavy lifting, straining, and high-impact activity for at least 2 weeks after orbital surgery or significant orbital trauma; longer for patients with known orbital varix or vascular malformation. Resume light walking within 24-72 hours of an uncomplicated decompression as tolerated. Return to non-contact aerobic exercise around week 2-3, and to contact sports only after specialist clearance, typically at 6-12 weeks, with protective eyewear.
dailyManagement: Sleep with the head elevated 30-45 degrees during the first week after decompression to reduce orbital venous pressure
Apply cold compresses to the periorbital region for 15-20 minutes every 2-3 hours during the first 24-48 hours to limit swelling
Take prescribed eye drops and oral pressure-lowering medications on schedule until cleared by ophthalmology
Check vision and pupil symmetry in a mirror daily during the first week and report any drop in acuity or new pain immediately
Avoid blowing the nose forcefully when there is concomitant orbital floor or medial wall fracture — this can drive air into the orbit (orbital emphysema) and worsen pressure
Attend all scheduled ophthalmology and oculoplastic follow-up appointments to monitor optic nerve and motility recovery
prognosis: Outcome turns almost entirely on time to decompression and the duration of orbital pressure-related ischemia. With lateral canthotomy and inferior cantholysis performed within 60-90 minutes of vision loss, 80-90% of patients regain useful vision in the affected eye. Beyond 120 minutes, useful recovery falls toward zero when initial vision was at the no-light-perception level. Patients with retained light perception at presentation fare better than those with no light perception, regardless of timing. Long-term sequelae after successful decompression can include partial optic nerve damage with persistent visual field defects, dyschromatopsia, secondary glaucoma, restrictive strabismus and diplopia from orbital fat fibrosis, cosmetic asymmetry, ptosis, and lower lid malposition. Recurrence is uncommon in trauma cases once the source has clotted but is more likely in patients with anticoagulation, uncontrolled hypertension, or untreated vascular malformation, all of which require active control as part of long-term care.
recoveryTimeline: Pressure-related symptoms abate within minutes of successful canthotomy and cantholysis. Periorbital ecchymosis and chemosis peak at 48-72 hours and largely settle by 2-3 weeks. Visual acuity typically improves over the first 24-72 hours and may continue to recover incrementally over 3-6 months. Restrictive strabismus and diplopia, when present, often improve over 6-12 weeks but may require strabismus surgery if not resolved by 6 months. Canthal reconstruction is typically deferred 1-3 months until tissues have settled.
complications: Permanent vision loss from ischemic optic neuropathy when decompression is delayed beyond 120 minutes
Central retinal artery or vein occlusion from sustained orbital compartment pressure
Secondary open-angle glaucoma from trauma to the trabecular meshwork
Restrictive strabismus and persistent diplopia from orbital fat fibrosis or rectus muscle injury
Enophthalmos and globe malposition once swelling resolves, particularly after large hematomas or associated fractures
Lower lid malposition, ectropion, and canthal asymmetry after canthotomy if reconstruction is delayed or poorly executed
Recurrent hemorrhage when the underlying vascular lesion or coagulation disorder is not controlled

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Patient Education
supportResources.description: Authoritative patient-facing information from the US ophthalmology specialty body.

supportResources.name: American Society of Ophthalmic Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery
supportResources.description: Find ASOPRS-trained oculoplastic surgeons for orbital and lid emergencies and longitudinal care.

supportResources.name: Royal College of Ophthalmologists — Patient Information
supportResources.description: UK specialty body patient resources, including eye injury and trauma information.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute angle-closure glaucoma also produces a red painful eye, very high intraocular pressure, and decreased vision, but the globe is not proptotic, periorbital ecchymosis is absent, and the pupil is mid-dilated and fixed rather than displaying an afferent defect. The cornea is hazy from edema. Treatment is medical and laser iridotomy, not orbital decompression.

confusedWithConditions.name: Intracranial Hypertension (Papilledema)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Intracranial hypertension produces bilateral disc edema, headache, and transient visual obscurations but the eye is not tense or proptotic, periorbital ecchymosis is absent, and intraocular pressure is normal. Imaging shows raised intracranial pressure findings rather than an orbital collection.

confusedWithConditions.name: Carotid-Cavernous Fistula
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A carotid-cavernous fistula presents with proptosis, chemosis, and dilated conjunctival vessels but is characterized by an audible orbital bruit, pulsation of the globe, and arterialization of the conjunctival vessels. Vision loss is more gradual and angiography is diagnostic; treatment is endovascular.

confusedWithConditions.name: Orbital Cellulitis (post-septal infection)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Orbital cellulitis is an infection (usually from adjacent sinus disease) presenting with proptosis, restricted motility, and pain over hours to days with fever and raised inflammatory markers. The globe is tense from edema rather than blood and CT shows orbital fat stranding, abscess, or sinus opacification rather than a hyperdense hematoma.

relatedConditions.name: Hypertension
relatedConditions.relevance: Uncontrolled systolic hypertension is a leading cause of spontaneous orbital hemorrhage and of post-operative bleeding.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Sustained orbital pressure during orbital hemorrhage can cause secondary glaucoma through trabecular meshwork injury, and the differential diagnosis of acute red eye includes both.

relatedConditions.name: Intracranial Hypertension
relatedConditions.relevance: Another pressure-driven cause of acute vision loss with optic nerve dysfunction, sharing some diagnostic features but managed very differently.

relatedConditions.name: Leukemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute leukemia can present with spontaneous orbital hemorrhage, especially in children, and any unexplained spontaneous bleed should prompt hematologic work-up.

relatedConditions.name: Cerebral Aneurysm (Carotid-Cavernous Fistula)
relatedConditions.relevance: Vascular causes of acute proptosis and orbital congestion overlap clinically with orbital hemorrhage, particularly carotid-cavernous fistulas.

faqs.question: What is orbital hemorrhage?
faqs.answer: Orbital hemorrhage is bleeding inside the bony eye socket. When blood collects behind the eyeball it raises pressure in the closed orbital compartment and can compress the optic nerve. It is treated as an emergency when there is proptosis, a tense eye, and decreased vision.

faqs.question: Why is orbital hemorrhage a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: The orbit is a closed bony space, so even a small amount of blood quickly raises pressure. Once pressure exceeds retinal artery perfusion, the optic nerve and retina lose blood supply and irreversible damage starts within 60 to 90 minutes.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of orbital compartment syndrome?
faqs.answer: Severe orbital pain, an eye that bulges forward, a tense globe, decreased vision, loss of red color saturation, and a relative afferent pupillary defect are key warning signs. Intraocular pressure above 40 mmHg after trauma, surgery, or a retrobulbar injection requires immediate assessment.

faqs.question: What causes orbital hemorrhage?
faqs.answer: Main causes are facial trauma, orbital fractures, retrobulbar or peribulbar anesthesia, oculoplastic surgery, anticoagulation, uncontrolled hypertension, valsalva straining, and rupture of orbital vascular malformations. Bleeding disorders and leukemia can also cause spontaneous orbital hemorrhage.

faqs.question: How is orbital hemorrhage diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical when the eye is proptotic, tense, and vision is dropping. Intraocular pressure, color vision, and pupillary reflex are tested. Non-contrast CT of the orbits confirms the location of bleeding once the eye has been decompressed or when vision is not immediately threatened.

faqs.question: What is a lateral canthotomy and cantholysis?
faqs.answer: It is a bedside surgical procedure that releases the lateral corner of the eyelids and the lower limb of the lateral canthal tendon. This decompresses the orbit by allowing the eye to move forward, dropping pressure within seconds. It is the definitive treatment for orbital compartment syndrome.

faqs.question: How quickly does treatment need to happen?
faqs.answer: Outcomes are best when decompression occurs within 60 to 90 minutes of vision loss. Roughly 80 to 90 percent of patients regain useful vision in that window. Delays beyond 120 minutes with severe vision loss are linked to very poor recovery.

faqs.question: Can vision return after orbital hemorrhage?
faqs.answer: Yes, in most patients treated within the first 60 to 90 minutes. Useful vision returns in about 80 to 90 percent of cases with timely decompression. Partial recovery is possible later when some light perception was preserved at presentation. Delayed care is linked to permanent loss.

faqs.question: Is orbital hemorrhage common after eye injections?
faqs.answer: Retrobulbar and peribulbar anesthetic injections cause orbital hemorrhage in roughly 0.04 to 3 percent of procedures, depending on technique and needle type. Sub-Tenon and topical anesthesia have largely replaced retrobulbar blocks for routine cataract surgery to reduce this risk.

faqs.question: Can blepharoplasty cause orbital hemorrhage?
faqs.answer: Yes, but it is rare. Sight-threatening retrobulbar hemorrhage occurs in about 1 in 25,000 blepharoplasty procedures and is the most often cited cause of blindness after cosmetic eyelid surgery. Risk increases with antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy and uncontrolled blood pressure.

faqs.question: Should I stop blood thinners before eyelid surgery?
faqs.answer: Decisions about stopping antiplatelet or anticoagulant medication before oculoplastic surgery are individualized based on thrombotic risk. Many low-risk patients pause aspirin or DOACs for several days under medical supervision, while higher-risk patients may need bridging.

faqs.question: What is the difference between orbital hemorrhage and orbital cellulitis?
faqs.answer: Orbital cellulitis is an infection that produces proptosis, restricted eye movement, fever, and raised inflammatory markers over hours to days. Orbital hemorrhage produces similar bulging and pain but appears suddenly after trauma, surgery, or anticoagulation, without fever.

faqs.question: Can orbital hemorrhage happen spontaneously without injury?
faqs.answer: Yes. Spontaneous orbital hemorrhage can occur with anticoagulation, uncontrolled hypertension, vigorous valsalva or vomiting, and orbital vascular malformations such as varix, lymphangioma, or AVM. It can also be a presenting feature of leukemia or other bleeding disorders.

faqs.question: Are children affected by orbital hemorrhage?
faqs.answer: Children can develop orbital hemorrhage from trauma, sinus-related vascular injury, lymphangioma rupture, or as a presenting feature of acute leukemia. Subperiosteal hematomas under the orbital roof are common in pediatric trauma. Workup for an underlying cause is essential if spontaneous.

faqs.question: What complications can occur after orbital hemorrhage?
faqs.answer: Complications include permanent vision loss from optic nerve ischemia, central retinal artery or vein occlusion, secondary glaucoma, persistent diplopia, enophthalmos, lower lid malposition, and recurrent hemorrhage when the underlying cause is not corrected.

faqs.question: What is the role of CT scanning in orbital hemorrhage?
faqs.answer: Non-contrast CT of the orbits confirms the location and size of the hematoma, identifies fractures, and shows posterior tenting of the globe or stretched optic nerve. When orbital compartment syndrome is clinically obvious, decompression is performed before imaging.

faqs.question: How is orbital pressure measured?
faqs.answer: Intraocular pressure is measured at the bedside using a Tono-Pen, iCare device, or Goldmann applanation tonometer. Intraorbital pressure itself is inferred from clinical signs and intraocular pressure. An IOP above 40 mmHg with vision changes is a key threshold for decompression.

faqs.question: Will I need surgery after lateral canthotomy?
faqs.answer: Many patients need only the initial canthotomy and cantholysis plus medical therapy. Additional surgery may be required to drain a persistent hematoma, repair fractures, reconstruct the lateral canthus, or treat an underlying vascular malformation, guided by follow-up.

faqs.question: What does recovery look like after orbital hemorrhage?
faqs.answer: Periorbital bruising and swelling peak within 48 to 72 hours and settle over 2 to 3 weeks. Vision often improves within the first 24 to 72 hours and may continue to recover over months. Most patients return to normal activity by 4 to 6 weeks.

faqs.question: Can orbital hemorrhage recur?
faqs.answer: Recurrence is uncommon in trauma cases once the source vessel clots, but is more likely with anticoagulation, uncontrolled hypertension, or untreated orbital vascular malformations. Controlling these drivers and avoiding valsalva during recovery reduces recurrence risk.

faqs.question: Is there a way to prevent orbital hemorrhage?
faqs.answer: Risk is reduced by using sub-Tenon or topical instead of retrobulbar anesthesia, optimizing anticoagulation and blood pressure before elective surgery, wearing protective eyewear in sports, using seatbelts, and treating known orbital vascular malformations electively.

faqs.question: When should I go to the emergency department?
faqs.answer: Go to the nearest emergency department immediately for any sudden eye pain with bulging, vision change, or eyelid bruising after trauma, eye surgery, or a retrobulbar injection. The window to save sight is short, often under two hours from the start of vision loss.
